Introduction to SQL

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
    echo = TRUE,
    fig.align = "center",
    fig.dim = c(7, 5),
    fig.pos = "H",
    message = FALSE,
    warning = FALSE,
    error = FALSE,
    include = TRUE
)

library(DBI)
# Una librería alternativa a `RPostgres` es `RPostgreSQL`,
# pero parece que ya no se le da mucho mantenimiento.
library(RPostgres)

# Para establecer la conexión
conexion = dbConnect(
  drv = Postgres(),
  dbname = "films",
  host = "localhost",
  port = 5432,
  user = "postgres",
  password = "100899"
)

knitr::opts_knit$set(
  # Límite del número de filas que se imprimen
  sql.max.print = 10
)

# Conexión por default y quitamos la caption de la tabla regresada por la query
knitr::opts_chunk$set(connection = conexion, 
                      tab.cap = NA)
dbListTables(conexion)
[1] "films"   "people"  "reviews" "roles"  
dbListFields(conexion, "people")
[1] "id"        "name"      "birthdate" "deathdate"
dbListFields(conexion, "films")
[1] "id"            "title"         "release_year"  "country"      
[5] "duration"      "language"      "certification" "gross"        
[9] "budget"

SQL is the most popular language for turning raw data stored in a database into actionable insights. Using a films database, you’ll learn how to filter, compare, and summarize data with SQL. You’ll also get a taste of aggregate functions, sorting, grouping, and how to present data neatly. Accompanied at every step with hands-on practice queries, this course teaches you everything you need to know to analyze data using your own SQL code today!

1 Queries

We will work with a films database containing four tables: films, reviews, people, and roles. Our database schema, pictured here, shows the table names, field names, and data types.

1.1 COUNT()

COUNT(*) tells you how many records are in a table. However, if you want to count the number of non-missing values in a particular field, you can call COUNT() on just that field.

-- Count the number of records in the people table:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS count_records 
FROM people;
count_records
8397 
-- Count the number of birthdates in the people table:
SELECT COUNT(birthdate) AS count_birthdate
FROM people;
count_birthdate
6152 
-- Count the records for languages and countries represented in the films table:
SELECT COUNT(language) AS count_languages, COUNT(country) AS count_countries
FROM films
count_languages count_countries
4957 4966

1.2 DISTINCT

Often query results will include many duplicate values. You can use the DISTINCT keyword to select the unique values from a field. This might be useful if, for example, you’re interested in knowing which languages are represented in the films table.

-- Return the unique countries from the films table
SELECT DISTINCT country
FROM films;
resultado
-- Count the distinct countries from the films table
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT country) AS count_distinct_countries
FROM films;
count_distinct_countries
64

SQL code is processed differently than other programming languages in that you need to let the processor know where to pull the data from before making selections.

It’s essential to know your code’s order of execution compared to the order it is written in to understand what results you’ll get from your query and how to fix any errors that may come up.

Si en el nombre de un campo hay espacios (por ejemplo, release year), tenemos que encerrar el nombre entre comillas cuando escribamos una query ("release year").

1.3 WHERE

-- Select film_ids and imdb_score with an imdb_score over 7.0
SELECT film_id, imdb_score
FROM reviews
WHERE imdb_score > 7;
film_id imdb_score
3934 7.1
74 7.6
1254 8.0
4841 8.1
3252 7.2
1181 7.3
3929 7.1
3298 7.4
2744 7.4
4707 7.4 
-- Select film_ids and facebook_likes for ten records with less than 1000 likes 
SELECT film_id, facebook_likes
FROM reviews
WHERE facebook_likes < 1000
LIMIT 10;
film_id facebook_likes
3405 0
478 491
74 930
740 0
2869 689
1181 0
2020 0
2312 912
1820 872
831 975 
-- Count the records with at least 100,000 votes
SELECT COUNT(num_votes) AS films_over_100K_votes
FROM reviews
WHERE num_votes >= 100000;
films_over_100k_votes
1211

WHERE can also filter string values.

-- Count the Spanish-language films
SELECT COUNT(language) AS count_spanish
FROM films
WHERE language = 'Spanish';
count_spanish
40

There are 40 Spanish-language films in this table.

1.4 AND, OR, BETWEEN

-- Select the title and release_year for all German-language films released before 2000
SELECT title, release_year
FROM films
WHERE language = 'German' AND release_year < 2000
title release_year
Metropolis 1927
Pandora’s Box 1929
The Torture Chamber of Dr. Sadism 1967
Das Boot 1981
Run Lola Run 1998
Aimee & Jaguar 1999 
-- Update the query to see all German-language films released after 2000
SELECT title, release_year
FROM films
WHERE release_year > 2000
    AND language = 'German';
resultado
-- Select all records for German-language films released after 2000 and before 2010
SELECT *
FROM films
WHERE release_year > 2000
    AND release_year < 2010
    AND language = 'German';
resultado

This time you’ll write a query to get the title and release_year of films released in 1990 or 1999, which were in English or Spanish and took in more than $2,000,000 gross.

SELECT title, release_year
FROM films
WHERE (release_year = 1990 OR release_year = 1999)
    AND (language = 'English' OR language = 'Spanish')
    AND gross > 2000000;
resultado

Let’s use BETWEEN with AND on the films database to get the title and release_year of all Spanish-language or French-language films released between 1990 and 2000 (inclusive) with budgets over $100 million.

SELECT title, release_year
FROM films
WHERE release_year BETWEEN 1990 AND 2000
    AND budget > 100000000
    AND (language = 'Spanish' OR language = 'French');
title release_year
Les couloirs du temps: Les visiteurs II 1998
Tango 1998

1.5 LIKE, NOT LIKE, IN

The LIKE and NOT LIKE operators can be used to find records that either match or do not match a specified pattern, respectively. They can be coupled with the wildcards % and _. The % will match zero or many characters, and _ will match a single character. This is useful when you want to filter text, but not to an exact word.

-- Select the names that start with B
SELECT name
FROM people
WHERE name LIKE 'B%';
resultado
SELECT name
FROM people
-- Select the names that have r as the second letter
WHERE name LIKE '_r%';
resultado
SELECT name
FROM people
-- Select names that don't start with A
WHERE name NOT LIKE 'A%';
resultado

Our results still had names that started with Á with an accent, showing that we need to be specific with our filtering criteria.

You now know you can query multiple conditions using the IN operator and a set of parentheses.

-- Find the title and release_year for all films over two hours in length released in 1990 and 2000
SELECT title, release_year
FROM films
WHERE release_year IN (1990, 2000)
    AND duration > 120;
resultado
-- Find the title and language of all films in English, Spanish, and French
SELECT title, language
FROM films
WHERE language IN ('English', 'Spanish', 'French');
resultado
-- Find the title, certification, and language all films certified NC-17 or R that are in English, Italian, or Greek
SELECT title, certification, language
FROM films
WHERE certification IN ('NC-17', 'R')
    AND language IN ('English', 'Italian', 'Greek');
resultado

Time for a little challenge. So far, your SQL vocabulary includes COUNT(), DISTINCT, LIMIT, WHERE, OR, AND, BETWEEN, LIKE, NOT LIKE, and IN. In this exercise, you will try to use some of these together.

-- Count the unique titles
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT title) AS nineties_english_films_for_teens
FROM films
-- Filter to release_years to between 1990 and 1999
WHERE release_year BETWEEN 1990 AND 1999
-- Filter to English-language films
    AND language = 'English'
-- Narrow it down to G, PG, and PG-13 certifications
    AND certification IN ('G', 'PG', 'PG-13');
nineties_english_films_for_teens
310

This filter tells us we have 310 films that the 90’s obsessed teenagers can enjoy.

1.6 IS NULL, IS NOT NULL

-- List all film titles with missing budgets
SELECT title AS no_budget_info
FROM films
WHERE budget IS NULL;
resultado

Los siguientes dos chunks regresan lo mismo:

-- Count the number of films we have language data for
SELECT COUNT(language) AS count_language_known
FROM films;
count_language_known
4957 
-- Count the number of films we have language data for
SELECT COUNT(language) AS count_language_known
FROM films
WHERE language IS NOT NULL;
count_language_known
4957

En efecto, COUNT() solo cuenta las entradas que no tengan missing values.

2 Aggregate functions

2.1 AVG(), SUM(), MIN(), MAX(), COUNT()

-- Query the sum of film durations
SELECT SUM(duration) AS total_duration
FROM films;
total_duration
534882 
-- Calculate the average duration of all films
SELECT AVG(duration) AS average_duration
FROM films;
average_duration
107.9479 
-- Find the latest release_year
SELECT MAX(release_year) AS latest_year
FROM films;
latest_year
2016 
-- Find the duration of the shortest film
SELECT MIN(duration) AS shortest_film
FROM films;
shortest_film
7

When combining aggregate functions with WHERE, you get a powerful tool that allows you to get more granular with your insights, for example, to get the total budget of movies made from the year 2010 onwards.

This combination is useful when you only want to summarize a subset of your data. In your film-industry role, as an example, you may like to summarize each certification category to compare how they each perform or if one certification has a higher average budget than another.

-- Calculate the sum of gross from the year 2000 or later
SELECT SUM(gross) AS total_gross
FROM films
WHERE release_year >= 2000;
total_gross
150900926358 
-- Calculate the average gross of films that start with A
SELECT AVG(gross) AS avg_gross_A
FROM films
WHERE title LIKE 'A%';
avg_gross_a
47893236 
-- Calculate the lowest gross film in 1994
SELECT MIN(gross) AS lowest_gross
FROM films
WHERE release_year = 1994;
lowest_gross
125169 
-- Calculate the highest gross film released between 2000-2012
SELECT MAX(gross) AS highest_gross
FROM films
WHERE release_year BETWEEN 2000 AND 2012;
highest_gross
760505847

2.2 ROUND()

Aggregate functions work great with numerical values; however, these results can sometimes get unwieldy when dealing with long decimal values. Luckily, SQL provides you with the ROUND() function to tame these long decimals.

-- Round the average number of facebook_likes to one decimal place
SELECT ROUND(AVG(facebook_likes), 1) AS avg_facebook_likes
FROM reviews;
avg_facebook_likes
7802.9

A useful thing you can do with ROUND() is have a negative number as the decimal place parameter. This can come in handy if your manager only needs to know the average number of facebook_likes to the hundreds since granularity below one hundred likes won’t impact decision making. Social media plays a significant role in determining success. If a movie trailer is posted and barely gets any likes, the movie itself may not be successful. Remember how 2020’s “Sonic the Hedgehog” movie got a revamp after the public saw the trailer?

-- Calculate the average budget rounded to the thousands
SELECT ROUND(AVG(budget), -3) AS avg_budget_thousands
FROM films;
avg_budget_thousands
39903000

2.3 Arithmetic

SELECT 1 + 1
?column?
2

Se recomienda utilizar paréntesis:

SELECT (2 * 3)
?column?
6

La división nos regresa enteros.

SELECT (1 / 4)
?column?
0

A menos que

SELECT (1.0 / 4.0)
?column?
0.25

Aliasing can be a lifesaver, especially as we start to do more complex SQL queries with multiple criteria. Aliases help you keep your code clean and readable. For example, if you want to find the MAX() value of several fields without aliasing, you’ll end up with the result with several columns called max and no idea which is which. You can fix this with aliasing.

-- Calculate the title and duration_hours from films
SELECT title, (duration / 60.0) AS duration_hours
FROM films;
resultado
-- Calculate the percentage of people who are no longer alive
SELECT COUNT(deathdate) * 100.0 / COUNT(*) AS percentage_dead
FROM people;
percentage_dead
9.372395 
-- Find the number of decades in the films table
SELECT (MAX(release_year) - MIN(release_year)) / 10.0 AS number_of_decades
FROM films;
number_of_decades
10

Now you know that our films table covers films released over one hundred years.

-- Round duration_hours to two decimal places
SELECT title, ROUND(duration / 60.0, 2) AS duration_hours
FROM films;
resultado

3 Sorting and Grouping

3.1 ORDER BY

Orden de ejecución:

  1. FROM
  2. WHERE
  3. SELECT
  4. ORDER BY
  5. LIMIT
-- Select name from people and sort alphabetically
SELECT name
FROM people
ORDER BY name;
resultado

Por default los ordena de manera ascendente:

-- Select name from people and sort alphabetically
SELECT name
FROM people
ORDER BY name ASC;
resultado

Podemos ordenarlos de manera descendente con DESC:

-- Select name from people and sort alphabetically
SELECT name
FROM people
ORDER BY name DESC;
resultado
-- Select the title and duration from longest to shortest film
SELECT title, duration
FROM films
ORDER BY duration;
resultado

No es necesario incluir el campo que se usa para ordenar las filas:

-- Select the title and duration from longest to shortest film
SELECT title
FROM films
ORDER BY duration;
resultado

ORDER BY can also be used to sort on multiple fields. It will sort by the first field specified, then sort by the next, and so on. La segunda columna por la que se ordenan las filas puede verse como un “tie breaker”.

-- Select the release year, duration, and title sorted by release year and duration
SELECT release_year, duration, title
FROM films
ORDER BY release_year, duration;
resultado
-- Select the certification, release year, and title sorted by certification and release year
SELECT certification, release_year, title 
FROM films
ORDER BY certification, release_year DESC;
resultado

The second column you order on only steps in when the first column has been ordered.

3.2 GROUP BY

GROUP BY is a SQL keyword that allows you to group and summarize results with the additional use of aggregate functions. For example, films can be grouped by the certification and language before counting the film titles in each group. This allows you to see how many films had a particular certification and language grouping.

Orden de ejecución:

  1. FROM
  2. GROUP BY
  3. SELECT y creación de alias
  4. ORDER BY. Podemos hacer referencia a los alias
-- Find the release_year and film_count of each year
SELECT release_year, COUNT(title) AS film_count
FROM films
GROUP BY release_year;
resultado
-- Find the release_year and average duration of films for each year
SELECT release_year, AVG(duration) AS avg_duration
FROM films
GROUP BY release_year;
resultado

GROUP BY becomes more powerful when used across multiple fields or combined with ORDER BY and LIMIT.

-- Find the release_year, country, and max_budget, then group and order by release_year and country
SELECT release_year, country, MAX(budget) AS max_budget
FROM films
GROUP BY release_year, country
ORDER BY release_year, country;
resultado

Año con mayor diversidad de idiomas: 2016

SELECT release_year, COUNT(DISTINCT language) AS language_count
FROM films
GROUP BY release_year
ORDER BY language_count DESC;
resultado

3.3 HAVING

HAVING works similarly to WHERE in that it is a filtering clause, with the difference that HAVING filters grouped data. Filtering grouped data can be especially handy when working with a large dataset. When working with thousands or even millions of rows, HAVING will allow you to filter for just the group of data you want, such as films over two hours in length!

Orden de ejecución:

  1. FROM
  2. WHERE
  3. GROUP BY
  4. HAVING. Es como un WHERE para datos agrupados. Notemos que WHERE se ejecuta antes de GROUP BY, por lo que no podemos utilizar esta keyword para datos agrupados; sin embargo, HAVING se ejecuta después de hacer la agrupación, así que esta keyword sustituye a WHERE para datos agrupados. Notemos además que se ejecuta antes de SELECT, así que no podemos utilizar aliases como con ORDER BY
  5. SELECT
  6. ORDER BY
  7. LIMIT

Ejemplos de preguntas que podríamos responder:

¿Qué películas salieron en el año 2000?

SELECT title
FROM films
WHERE release_year = 2000;
resultado

¿En qué años la duración promedio de las películas era mayor a dos horas?

SELECT release_year
FROM films
GROUP BY release_year
HAVING AVG(duration) > 120;
resultado

Which countries (or country) have the most varied film certifications?

-- Select the country and distinct count of certification as certification_count
SELECT country, COUNT(DISTINCT certification) AS certification_count
FROM films
-- Group by country
GROUP BY country
-- Filter results to countries with more than 10 different certifications
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT certification) > 10;
resultado

What countries have the highest average film budgets?

-- Select the country and average_budget from films
SELECT country, AVG(budget) AS average_budget
FROM films
-- Group by country
GROUP BY country
-- Filter to countries with an average_budget of more than one billion
HAVING AVG(budget) > 1000000000
-- Order by descending order of the aggregated budget
ORDER BY average_budget DESC;
resultado

These budgets are pretty standard for their local currency.

Average budget and gross earnings for films each year after 1990 if the average budget is greater than 60 million.

SELECT release_year, AVG(budget) AS avg_budget, AVG(gross) AS avg_gross
FROM films
WHERE release_year > 1990
GROUP BY release_year
HAVING AVG(budget) > 60000000
ORDER BY avg_gross DESC
resultado
